highlight:
[future:Flitters]. Autonomous local kite-sails — the robo-ubers. Short-range, single-passenger or small-cargo, operating within and between settlements. Ubiquitous. Taking a flitter is like calling a cab.

Personal craft. Sub-orbital or space-capable vessels owned by individuals. Upper-middle-class luxury — the equivalent of owning a nice sailboat. Comfortable enough for extended trips, not built for deep Shear or interplanetary hauls.

Commercial haulers and passenger vessels. The Vantara fleet and independent operators. Cargo haulers running the Keel and other trade lanes. Passenger liners between major settlements. Fast couriers. This is the backbone of system commerce.

Shear-rated vessels. Salvage crews, mining rigs, survey ships. Carry additional structural-band arrays for reality stabilization shielding. The Shear's resonance distortion can interfere with kinetic fields if local physics aren't anchored.

Military. Warships and patrol vessels — remnants of the Contested Reach and Bitter Reach eras, maintained because piracy is ongoing. Vantara operates the largest private armed escort fleet. Various factions maintain their own. Kinetic fields can be used offensively, which is technically possible and socially frowned upon.

highlight: Every buoy deployed is stillwater that isn't preserving history. The Displacement Council maintains the network. The Conclave supplies the stillwater. The tension between these two needs — containment and preservation — is one of the system's quietest and most consequential ongoing negotiations.
comment: future beat: some removes a bouy and tries to look inside as if the still water held memories. what they find is instructive as to the nature of the bloom

highlight: This matters because a common enemy unifies differently than a common problem — enemies create permanent alliances, problems create temporary ones.
comment: i dont think this is accurate. the difference is the kind of result the cooperation achives, not the longevity of the alliances

highlight:
The orbital rings were built from a crystalline material — now called ringglass — that vibrates at stable, predictable frequencies when energized. The original builders used this property to move power, data, and physical force through the ring structure without cables or broadcast towers. The crystal itself was the medium. Tune a section of ringglass to the right frequency, and it would pass energy along to the next section, and the next, like a vibration traveling down a wire.

When the rings shattered, the ringglass didn't stop vibrating. It scattered — across the surface of Kaleidos, into orbit, into the soil and water — and it kept resonating. Not in the organized, purposeful way the builders intended, but in a diffuse, ambient hum that permeates the environment anywhere ringglass is present. Which, after 130 years of settling debris, is almost everywhere on Kaleidos and throughout the ring habs.

That ambient energy is resonance. It's not magic. It's the residual output of an engineered system running without supervision.
comment: this section needs to be reworked slightly - ringglass is not the source of resonance, it is the most stable expression of it. but we state elsewhere that resonance is ambient - and we want to be able to use it on planets other the kaledios. ringglass is an amplifier, not a source
